stem:
A 30-year-old primary school teacher presents to your neurology clinic with a 6-month history of left-sided weakness and intermittent right hand tremor. The symptoms began 2 days after a stressful complaint from a parent at work. The weakness fluctuates and is worse when observed during examination. She has not had falls and has continued to drive.

On examination, Hoover's sign is positive (her left hip extension force normalises when she is asked to flex the contralateral hip against resistance). The right hand tremor entrains to a tapping rhythm she is asked to perform with the left hand. Sensory examination shows a non-anatomical splitting at the midline. Power graded as 3/5 in the left leg drops away with give-way pattern. MRI brain, cervical spine MRI, and nerve conduction studies are reported normal.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Hoover sign positive, tremor entrainment positive, midline splitting of sensory level, give-way weakness, normal tone, tendon reflexes preserved, no clonus, plantars downgoing
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain with contrast normal, MRI cervical spine normal, EMG and nerve conduction studies normal, FBC and UEC within normal limits, ANA negative, B12 and folate within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer to a forensic psychiatrist to assess for malingering and benefit-seeking behaviour as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Malingering requires identifiable secondary gain and intentional symptom production; FND is involuntary. Diagnosing malingering on the basis of inconsistency is incorrect and stigmatising. (Source: BMJ Best Practice FND)

options.text: Refer for repeat MRI brain in 3 months and observe with no diagnostic label as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action as the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Repeat imaging without an evolving clinical picture does not change management and delays appropriate treatment; FND has positive clinical features and can be communicated as a clear diagnosis. (Source: BMJ Best Practice FND)

options.text: Explain that the diagnosis is functional neurological disorder based on positive clinical signs, and refer for symptom-specific physiotherapy and FND-informed psychological therapy
options.rationale: FND is diagnosed by positive features (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, non-anatomical sensory level) not solely by exclusion. The explanation as a problem with how the brain controls movement (not imagined or feigned), with referral to symptom-specific physiotherapy and FND-informed psychological therapy, has the strongest evidence base and improves outcomes. (Source: BMJ Best Practice FND)

options.text: Commence amitriptyline 50 mg nocte for somatoform pain as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action as the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: There is no role for amitriptyline in motor FND; first-line is positive diagnosis, education, and symptom-specific physiotherapy and psychological therapy. (Source: BMJ Best Practice FND)

options.text: Commence prednisolone 50 mg daily for empirical inflammatory neurological treatment as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Steroid therapy has no role in FND and risks adverse effects with no benefit; immunotherapy is reserved for confirmed autoimmune neurological disease. (Source: BMJ Best Practice FND)

explanation.summary: Functional neurological disorder is now a positive-feature diagnosis: Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, non-anatomical sensory loss, dissociative seizure semiology. Communicating a clear positive diagnosis (not exclusion-by-default) and referring to FND-informed physiotherapy and psychological therapy improves outcomes. Malingering and somatisation are different diagnostic constructs. Imaging surveillance and empirical immunotherapy or amitriptyline have no role.
key_points: FND diagnosed by positive signs, not exclusion only
Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way are highly specific
Treatment: FND physiotherapy + FND-informed psychological therapy
Distinguished from malingering (intentional, secondary gain)
explanation.pearls: Hoover sign: the patient's apparently weak hip extension force normalises when they flex the contralateral hip against resistance, indicating attentional rather than structural weakness.

stem:
A 76-year-old man on the surgical ward day 3 post-emergency laparotomy for perforated diverticulitis becomes increasingly agitated overnight. He pulls at his lines, tries to climb out of bed, and shouts at staff. His past history includes mild cognitive impairment, well-controlled hypertension, and ischaemic heart disease (LVEF 45%, no recent events). Verbal de-escalation and reorientation have not helped, and he has attempted to strike the nurse.

On examination he is disoriented to time and place but recognises his daughter. He has no fever, vitals are stable, and his most recent ECG today shows sinus rhythm with QTc 450 ms. Recent renal function and electrolytes are within normal limits. He is on regular paracetamol, oxycodone 5 mg PRN, ceftriaxone, and metronidazole.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92, BP 142/86 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Disoriented to time and place, attentional deficit on serial 7s, recognises family, no focal neurology, no nuchal rigidity
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 138 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 4.2 mmol/L, urea 6.0 mmol/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, glucose 6.4 mmol/L; ECG sinus rhythm QTc 450 ms; chest X-ray no consolidation; urinalysis nitrites positive, leukocytes 2+
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol 1 g 6-hourly, oxycodone 5 mg PRN, IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily, IV metronidazole 500 mg 8-hourly

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Haloperidol 5 mg intramuscularly now, repeat every 30 minutes as required
options.rationale: High-dose IM haloperidol 5 mg is inappropriate first-line in a frail elderly patient with cardiac history and a QTc of 450 ms; low-dose oral or sublingual antipsychotic is preferred. AU guidelines reserve high-dose IM haloperidol for severe behavioural disturbance in younger patients with no cardiovascular comorbidity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic)

options.text: Lorazepam 1 mg orally now, repeat after 60 minutes if required
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines worsen delirium in the elderly and increase falls and respiratory depression risk; they are reserved for delirium related to alcohol or sedative withdrawal or where antipsychotics are contraindicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic)

options.text: Olanzapine wafer 2.5 mg orally now, repeat after 60 minutes if required
options.rationale: AU Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic recommend low-dose oral olanzapine 2.5-5 mg or risperidone 0.25-0.5 mg as first-line pharmacotherapy for acute agitation in delirium when non-pharmacological measures fail and there is risk of harm; the wafer formulation is suitable when oral cooperation is uncertain. Avoid benzodiazepines as monotherapy because they can worsen delirium except in alcohol or sedative withdrawal. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic)

options.text: Promethazine 25 mg intramuscularly now as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Promethazine adds anticholinergic burden and worsens delirium in the elderly; it is not appropriate for behavioural disturbance in this setting. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic)

options.text: Diazepam 10 mg intravenously now as the proposed approach
options.rationale: High-dose IV diazepam in an elderly patient with delirium increases respiratory depression and falls risk and prolongs delirium; not appropriate here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Hyperactive delirium in an elderly post-operative patient with cardiovascular comorbidity calls for low-dose oral atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine 2.5 mg wafer or risperidone 0.25-0.5 mg). Benzodiazepines worsen delirium except in alcohol/sedative withdrawal. High-dose IM haloperidol risks QT prolongation and is not first-line in elderly cardiac patients. Address precipitants (sepsis from possible UTI, opioids, environment, sleep).
key_points: Low-dose olanzapine 2.5 mg wafer or risperidone 0.25-0.5 mg first-line
Avoid benzodiazepines unless alcohol/sedative withdrawal
Address precipitants (sepsis, pain, opioids, sensory deprivation)
Reassess QTc and EPS regularly
explanation.pearls: Antipsychotics in delirium are symptomatic only and do not shorten duration; identifying and treating the precipitant (here possibly UTI plus opioids) is the real intervention.

stem:
A 19-year-old woman is on day 3 of inpatient admission for severe anorexia nervosa. Admission BMI was 13.2 kg/m^2 with bradycardia 38, hypothermia 35.4 °C, and an ECG showing sinus bradycardia with prolonged QTc 480 ms. She has been started on the ward refeeding protocol at 20 kcal/kg/day with thiamine, multivitamin, and phosphate supplementation, with daily electrolyte and ECG monitoring.

This morning on day 3 her serum phosphate has dropped from 1.05 to 0.45 mmol/L and her magnesium has fallen from 0.85 to 0.62 mmol/L despite oral replacement. Her ECG today shows QTc 510 ms, sinus rhythm 42. She is alert but reports cramping and mild dyspnoea on minimal exertion. She has been compliant with the meal plan and supervised meals.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 42, BP 92/58 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 35.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Cachectic, dry mucous membranes, peripheral pulses palpable, no oedema, tendon reflexes preserved with prolonged relaxation phase
data_table.Investigations: Phosphate 0.45 mmol/L (0.75-1.50), magnesium 0.62 mmol/L (0.70-1.10), potassium 3.3 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), corrected calcium 2.18 mmol/L (2.10-2.60), glucose 4.2 mmol/L, sodium 134 mmol/L, ALT 78 U/L (<40), albumin 32 g/L, ECG sinus bradycardia 42 QTc 510 ms
data_table.Medications: Oral phosphate Sandoz 500 mg twice daily, oral magnesium aspartate 6.5 mmol twice daily, thiamine 300 mg daily, multivitamin, refeeding protocol 20 kcal/kg/day

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Increase caloric intake to 30 kcal/kg/day to ensure adequate refeeding progress as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action as part of routine inpatient care
options.rationale: Increasing rate during impending refeeding syndrome accelerates phosphate and magnesium drop and increases cardiac arrest risk. Rate is held until electrolytes stabilise. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders)

options.text: Stop refeeding entirely and recommence after 24 hours of fasting with electrolyte recheck as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action as part of routine inpatient care
options.rationale: Stopping refeeding entirely is the wrong branch; cessation drives further metabolic instability through ongoing glycogen depletion and starvation physiology. Electrolyte replacement plus holding rate is the appropriate approach. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders)

options.text: Hold caloric advancement at the current rate, give IV magnesium sulfate 10 mmol over 2 hours, IV potassium phosphate (typically 20-30 mmol over 4-6 hours via central line per inpatient pathway), repeat electrolytes 4-hourly, and consult intensive care
options.rationale: Falling phosphate and magnesium with QTc 510 ms and clinical symptoms (cramps, dyspnoea) is impending refeeding syndrome. AU practice is to HOLD caloric advancement (not stop refeeding), aggressively replace IV phosphate and magnesium, monitor electrolytes 4-hourly, and involve intensive care because of cardiac instability risk. Stopping feeds risks further metabolic catastrophe; advancing risks decompensation. The middle branch is the correct one. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders)

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg daily to assist weight gain and address anorexia cognitions as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action as part of routine inpatient care
options.rationale: Olanzapine has modest weight-gain effect in anorexia but is not indicated for acute refeeding syndrome management; the priority is electrolyte stabilisation. (Source: RANZCP Eating Disorders)

options.text: Discharge to outpatient eating disorder day program as her clinical engagement is good as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action as part of routine inpatient care
options.rationale: Discharge in active refeeding syndrome with QTc prolongation is unsafe; this is the highest-risk window for cardiac arrest and requires close inpatient monitoring. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders)

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome arises from intracellular shift of phosphate, potassium and magnesium when carbohydrate is reintroduced after starvation. The correct response is to HOLD caloric advancement at current rate (not stop, not advance) and aggressively replace IV phosphate and magnesium with frequent electrolyte and ECG monitoring. ICU involvement is appropriate when QTc lengthens or arrhythmia risk rises. Stopping feeds entirely worsens metabolic instability.
key_points: Refeeding syndrome: ↓phosphate, ↓magnesium, ↓potassium, fluid shifts
Hold caloric rate; do not stop or advance
IV replacement: phosphate 20-30 mmol, magnesium 10-20 mmol
ICU involvement if QTc prolongs or arrhythmia risk
explanation.pearls: The wrong-branch temptations in refeeding syndrome are both 'stop feeds' and 'advance feeds' - the correct branch is 'hold rate, replace IV, monitor 4-hourly'. AU practice mandates a structured protocol with intensive care involvement once QTc lengthens.

stem:
A 56-year-old man presents to your general practice with a 2-day history of a red, painful, hot patch on the medial aspect of his right shin. He recalls a minor abrasion gardening 4 days ago. He has type 2 diabetes managed with metformin (HbA1c 6.8% last month), no prior MRSA, and no penicillin allergy. He is systemically well, afebrile, and has a clear margin on the cellulitis that you outline with a marker.

On examination there is a 12 cm by 8 cm area of warmth, erythema, and tenderness on the medial right shin without lymphangitic streaking, abscess, or crepitus. Distal pulses are intact and sensation is preserved. There is no necrosis, bullae, or pain disproportionate to examination.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 84, BP 134/82 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: 12 x 8 cm area of warmth and erythema medial right shin, marker outlined, no abscess, no crepitus, intact pulses, normal sensation, no lymphangitic streaking
data_table.Investigations: Capillary glucose 8.2 mmol/L, recent HbA1c 6.8% (4.0-6.0 normal); no bloods done today

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral cefalexin 500 mg four times daily for 5 days as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Cefalexin is reasonable but at 500 mg QID covers streptococci adequately but has weaker beta-lactamase stability against community methicillin-susceptible S. aureus than flucloxacillin in AU practice. eTG prefers flucloxacillin first-line for non-purulent cellulitis without MRSA risk factors. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 1 g daily via hospital in the home for 5 days
options.rationale: IV antibiotics are indicated when systemic sepsis features are present (sepsis criteria, rapid progression, failure of oral therapy), in immunosuppressed patients, or for facial cellulitis; this patient is systemically well with limited disease and well-controlled diabetes. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral clindamycin 450 mg three times daily for 7 days as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Clindamycin is reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy or where MRSA cover is needed. It is not first-line in non-purulent cellulitis without MRSA risk factors. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg twice daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is used when MRSA is suspected or confirmed and is not standard first-line cover for streptococcal cellulitis where streptococci are the predominant organism. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5 days for this patient
options.rationale: eTG recommends oral flucloxacillin 500 mg every 6 hours for 5 days as first-line for non-purulent cellulitis in an adult without MRSA risk factors or severe sepsis; it covers Streptococcus pyogenes and methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus, the predominant causative organisms. Mark the erythema border and reassess at 48 hours. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: eTG first-line for non-purulent cellulitis in an adult without MRSA risk factors and without severe sepsis is oral flucloxacillin 500 mg every 6 hours for 5 days. Mark the margin and reassess at 48 hours; escalate to IV if no improvement, sepsis develops, or rapid progression. Clindamycin or TMP-SMX is for non-severe penicillin allergy or MRSA cover. IV therapy is reserved for systemic illness or facial/orbital cellulitis.
key_points: First-line non-purulent cellulitis: flucloxacillin 500 mg QID 5 days
Cefalexin is reasonable but second-choice in AU practice
TMP-SMX or clindamycin if MRSA risk or non-severe penicillin allergy
IV therapy: systemic illness, rapid progression, facial cellulitis
explanation.pearls: Marking the erythema border with a marker and reassessing at 48 hours is an underused practical bedside test that tells you whether the antibiotic is working before changing therapy.

stem:
A 68-year-old woman attends your GP clinic for review after a minimal-trauma distal radius fracture sustained 6 weeks ago. She is otherwise well, post-menopausal since age 51, with no fragility fractures previously. Her mother had a hip fracture at age 72. She is on no regular medications. Her renal function is normal (eGFR 88 mL/min/1.73 m^2), serum calcium 2.36 mmol/L, vitamin D 78 nmol/L, and PTH within normal limits.

DEXA scan shows lumbar spine T-score -2.7 and femoral neck T-score -2.5. FRAX 10-year hip fracture probability is 6.2% and major osteoporotic fracture 14.5%. She has good dental health with no upcoming invasive dental procedures. She prefers oral therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 132/82 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Well-healed distal radius scar, no kyphosis, no spinal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Calcium corrected 2.36 mmol/L (2.10-2.60), phosphate 1.05 mmol/L, vitamin D 78 nmol/L, PTH 4.6 pmol/L (1.6-6.9), eGFR 88 mL/min/1.73 m^2, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, DEXA L-spine T -2.7, F-neck T -2.5
data_table.Medications: Calcium 600 mg + vitamin D 800 IU daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence oral alendronate 70 mg once weekly with calcium and vitamin D, falls assessment, and rebook DEXA in 2 years
options.rationale: Postmenopausal woman with a minimal-trauma fracture plus DEXA T-score ≤-2.5 meets PBS-funded criteria for anti-resorptive therapy. Oral alendronate 70 mg weekly is first-line in patients with normal renal function and no high-risk dental procedures or oesophageal contraindication; calcium and vitamin D are maintained; falls assessment is added. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis)

options.text: Commence denosumab 60 mg subcutaneously every 6 months with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Denosumab is an alternative anti-resorptive and is preferred for renal impairment (eGFR <35) or oral bisphosphonate intolerance. With normal renal function and patient preference for oral therapy, oral alendronate is first-line. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis)

options.text: Commence teriparatide 20 mcg subcutaneously daily for 18 months with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Teriparatide is an anabolic agent reserved for severe osteoporosis with multiple vertebral fractures, very low T-score, or failure of anti-resorptive therapy; it is not first-line in this case. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis)

options.text: Continue calcium and vitamin D only and recheck DEXA in 12 months as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Calcium and vitamin D alone are insufficient when osteoporosis is established with a recent minimal-trauma fracture; PBS-funded pharmacotherapy is indicated and delay increases re-fracture risk. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis)

options.text: Commence menopausal hormone therapy with conjugated oestrogens and medroxyprogesterone as the proposed approach
options.rationale: MHT is no longer first-line for osteoporosis given thromboembolic, stroke, and breast cancer risks in older postmenopausal women, particularly more than 10 years from menopause as in this case. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis)

explanation.summary: A postmenopausal woman with minimal-trauma fracture plus DEXA T-score ≤-2.5 meets PBS criteria for anti-resorptive therapy. Oral alendronate 70 mg weekly is first-line in normal renal function with no dental contraindication. Denosumab is preferred for renal impairment or oral intolerance. Teriparatide is for severe disease. MHT is no longer first-line for osteoporosis in older women.
key_points: Minimal-trauma fracture + T ≤-2.5 = anti-resorptive eligible
First-line: alendronate 70 mg weekly if eGFR ≥35 and no dental contraindication
Denosumab if renal impairment or oral intolerance
Calcium 1000 mg/day + vitamin D 800-1000 IU/day
explanation.pearls: PBS osteoporosis criteria in AU include minimal-trauma fragility fracture in adults aged 70+ OR DEXA T-score ≤-2.5; either alone meets the eligibility threshold.

stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to your neurology clinic 3 weeks after acute onset of blurred vision and pain on movement of her right eye lasting 10 days, now resolving. There is no preceding viral illness, no recent travel, and no family history of demyelinating disease. She has a 6-month-old breastfed child and is otherwise well.

On examination today her visual acuity is 6/9 right and 6/6 left with a relative afferent pupillary defect on the right. Fundoscopy shows mild right optic disc pallor. Neurological examination is otherwise normal. MRI brain demonstrates three periventricular and one juxtacortical T2 hyperintense lesion with no enhancement on gadolinium; MRI cervical spine shows one short-segment lesion. CSF reveals oligoclonal bands positive in CSF, absent in serum.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.5 °C
data_table.Examination: Right visual acuity 6/9, left 6/6, right RAPD positive, mild right disc pallor, no internuclear ophthalmoplegia, no nystagmus, motor and sensory examination normal, no cerebellar signs
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain: 3 periventricular + 1 juxtacortical T2 lesions, no gadolinium enhancement; MRI cervical spine: 1 short-segment T2 lesion; CSF: oligoclonal bands positive in CSF (negative in serum), normal cell count, glucose, protein; aquaporin-4 IgG and MOG-IgG negative

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Vitamin B12 deficiency myelopathy with subacute combined degeneration
options.rationale: Subacute combined degeneration causes dorsal column and corticospinal tract signs, often peripheral neuropathy and megaloblastic anaemia. It does not cause optic neuritis with periventricular MRI lesions and OCBs. (Source: BMJ Best Practice MS)

options.text: Clinically isolated syndrome alone, not yet meeting MS criteria
options.rationale: CIS describes a first clinical event without dissemination in time evidence; here CSF oligoclonal bands plus DIS substitute for DIT under the 2017 McDonald criteria, making this MS rather than isolated CIS. (Source: 2017 McDonald Criteria)

options.text: Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) as the proposed approach
options.rationale: NMOSD typically presents with longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (3+ vertebral segments) or severe optic neuritis with aquaporin-4 IgG positivity; AQP4-IgG is negative here and lesion patterns are consistent with MS. (Source: International NMOSD Criteria)

options.text: MOG antibody disease with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: MOG-IgG is negative; MOGAD typically presents with bilateral optic neuritis, ADEM-like episodes, or longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis, with a different MRI pattern. (Source: MOGAD Criteria)

options.text: Multiple sclerosis (relapsing-remitting) by 2017 McDonald criteria
options.rationale: Dissemination in space is met by typical MRI lesions in two or more CNS locations (here periventricular and juxtacortical plus spinal). Dissemination in time is met by CSF-specific oligoclonal bands (acceptable substitute for a second clinical attack or enhancing lesion under 2017 McDonald criteria). The clinical syndrome of optic neuritis with positive lesion and OCBs supports RRMS diagnosis. (Source: 2017 McDonald Criteria)

explanation.summary: The 2017 McDonald criteria allow CSF-specific oligoclonal bands to substitute for dissemination in time. With one clinical attack (optic neuritis) and DIS demonstrated on MRI plus CSF OCBs, the diagnosis advances from CIS to relapsing-remitting MS, allowing earlier initiation of disease-modifying therapy. AQP4 and MOG antibody testing are mandatory in atypical presentations to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD respectively.
key_points: 2017 McDonald: OCBs substitute for DIT
DIS: lesions in ≥2 of 4 typical locations (periventricular, juxtacortical/cortical, infratentorial, spinal)
Test AQP4 and MOG to exclude alternatives
Earlier diagnosis = earlier DMT = better long-term outcome
explanation.pearls: The 2017 McDonald update shifted OCBs from a supportive feature to an accepted substitute for DIT, enabling MS diagnosis at the first clinical event in many patients and earlier DMT initiation.

stem:
A 52-year-old woman with established AChR antibody-positive myasthenia gravis on pyridostigmine 60 mg six times daily and prednisolone 20 mg daily is brought to the emergency department with worsening dyspnoea, dysphagia, and bilateral ptosis. She had a mild upper respiratory infection 5 days ago, and her treating neurologist increased her pyridostigmine from 4 to 6 doses daily 2 days ago.

In the emergency department she is anxious and breathless with shallow respirations. She has bilateral ptosis, weak shoulder abduction, and difficulty swallowing her saliva. Her pupils are 3 mm and reactive, mucous membranes are dry, and bowel sounds are normal. Arterial blood gas shows pH 7.32, pCO2 56 mmHg, pO2 78 mmHg on room air, with bedside FVC measured at 1.0 L (predicted 3.4 L).

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 108, BP 132/86 mmHg, RR 28, SpO2 92% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Bilateral ptosis, fatigable shoulder abduction, dysarthria, pooling secretions, paradoxical abdominal breathing, no fasciculations, pupils 3 mm reactive, dry mucous membranes
data_table.Investigations: ABG pH 7.32, pCO2 56 mmHg, pO2 78 mmHg, HCO3 28; FVC 1.0 L (predicted 3.4 L), single breath count 12; sodium 136 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, urea 5 mmol/L, troponin <5 ng/L, viral swab pending
data_table.Medications: Pyridostigmine 60 mg 6x daily (recently increased from 4x), prednisolone 20 mg daily, calcium and vitamin D

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Pulmonary embolism; commence therapeutic enoxaparin and arrange CTPA as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Pyrexia, normal troponin, and clinical features of bulbar weakness with ptosis are characteristic of myasthenic crisis, not PE; ABG shows hypercapnia (PE typically hypocapnia with type 1 respiratory failure). (Source: BMJ Best Practice MG)

options.text: Cholinergic crisis; administer atropine 0.6 mg IV and continue pyridostigmine as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Cholinergic crisis presents with miosis, fasciculations, hypersalivation, lacrimation, diarrhoea, and bradycardia (SLUDGE/miosis), which are absent here. Dry mucous membranes and normal pupil size point to myasthenic, not cholinergic, crisis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Neurology)

options.text: Myasthenic crisis; arrange ICU admission for non-invasive ventilation or intubation, withhold pyridostigmine temporarily, and commence plasmapheresis or IVIG
options.rationale: Bilateral ptosis with bulbar weakness, paradoxical breathing, hypercapnia (pCO2 56 mmHg) and FVC under 1 L (or 15 mL/kg) define myasthenic crisis. Initial trigger here was viral illness. Distinguishing from cholinergic crisis: pupils normal-sized, mucous membranes dry (not wet), no fasciculations, bowel sounds normal - all favouring myasthenic rather than cholinergic crisis. Treatment is airway protection (NIV or intubation), withholding pyridostigmine to remove ambiguity, and immunomodulation with plasmapheresis or IVIG. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Neurology)

options.text: Asthma exacerbation; administer nebulised salbutamol and IV hydrocortisone as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Asthma exacerbation does not cause bilateral ptosis, dysphagia, or fatigable shoulder weakness, and the respiratory failure here is from neuromuscular weakness not bronchoconstriction. Wheeze is absent. (Source: BMJ Best Practice MG)

options.text: Anxiety hyperventilation; reassure and arrange anxiolytic as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action as the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Hyperventilation would cause hypocapnia, not hypercapnia, and would not produce bilateral ptosis or bulbar features. The objective ABG and FVC exclude anxiety as cause. (Source: BMJ Best Practice MG)

explanation.summary: Myasthenic crisis: FVC <15 mL/kg or <1 L, weak cough, respiratory failure, often triggered by infection or medication. Cholinergic crisis (excess pyridostigmine): SLUDGE/miosis with fasciculations and bronchorrhoea. Pupil size and secretions are the bedside discriminators. Management of myasthenic crisis is airway/ventilation support, withholding pyridostigmine temporarily, and plasmapheresis or IVIG to remove circulating antibodies.
key_points: Myasthenic: normal pupils, dry membranes, no fasciculations
Cholinergic: miosis, fasciculations, SLUDGE
FVC <1 L or <15 mL/kg is crisis threshold
Withhold pyridostigmine, IVIG or plasmapheresis, ICU
explanation.pearls: Cholinergic crisis is rare in modern practice as pyridostigmine doses are usually conservative; assume myasthenic crisis until cholinergic features (miosis, fasciculations, bronchorrhoea) prove otherwise.

stem:
A 62-year-old man with long-standing hypertension and a 30-pack-year smoking history presents to the emergency department with sudden severe interscapular and chest pain that began 90 minutes ago while gardening. He describes it as tearing in quality, peaking immediately at onset. He has no nausea, no diaphoresis, and no exertional component. He has no known prior cardiovascular event.

In the resuscitation bay, his right arm BP is 168/96 mmHg and left arm BP is 132/82 mmHg. Pulse 88, regular. Chest auscultation reveals a soft early diastolic murmur best heard at the left sternal edge. ECG shows sinus rhythm with non-specific lateral T-wave flattening. Bedside troponin is below the rule-in threshold. Chest X-ray shows widening of the upper mediastinum. He is hypertensive, alert, and orientated.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP right arm 168/96 mmHg, left arm 132/82 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Soft early diastolic murmur left sternal edge, no carotid bruit, peripheral pulses intact bilaterally, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus, non-specific lateral T flattening; troponin <5 ng/L; chest X-ray widened mediastinum; D-dimer pending; FBC and creatinine within normal limits
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 8 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer for urgent coronary angiogram for suspected NSTEMI as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Coronary angiography with anticoagulation in dissection causes catastrophic bleeding; dissection must be excluded before any invasive coronary procedure even when troponin rises (which can occur with coronary ostia involvement in dissection). (Source: ESC Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Administer aspirin 300 mg, ticagrelor 180 mg, and IV heparin while awaiting troponin trend as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Dual antiplatelet therapy and anticoagulation in suspected aortic dissection cause catastrophic bleeding into the false lumen and pericardial space; ACS treatment is contraindicated until dissection is excluded. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular)

options.text: Bolus IV glyceryl trinitrate without rate control to lower blood pressure rapidly as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Vasodilators alone increase reflex tachycardia and aortic wall stress; beta-blockade must precede or accompany any vasodilator therapy to control rate and dP/dt before reducing BP. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular)

options.text: Arrange transthoracic echocardiogram and discharge pending outpatient follow-up as the proposed approach
options.rationale: TTE sensitivity for aortic dissection is limited (especially descending dissection) and discharge of a patient with classical dissection features is unsafe; CT aortogram or TOE is required urgently. (Source: ESC Aortic Dissection)

options.text: Urgent contrast-enhanced CT aortogram and immediate IV labetalol infusion targeting systolic BP 100-120 mmHg and heart rate under 60
options.rationale: Sudden tearing interscapular pain, blood pressure differential between arms over 20 mmHg, aortic regurgitation murmur and widened mediastinum strongly suggest acute aortic dissection. Urgent CT aortogram is the diagnostic modality of choice in the haemodynamically stable patient; meanwhile rapid IV beta-blockade (labetalol or esmolol) targeting heart rate under 60 and systolic BP 100-120 mmHg reduces aortic wall shear stress. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: Acute aortic dissection is suggested by sudden severe tearing chest/back pain, inter-arm BP differential over 20 mmHg, aortic regurgitation murmur, and widened mediastinum. CT aortogram is the diagnostic standard. Treatment targets rate first (HR under 60 with beta-blockade) then BP (systolic 100-120 mmHg). ACS therapy is contraindicated until dissection is excluded. Type A dissections require emergency cardiothoracic surgery; type B is medical unless complicated.
key_points: Pain quality + BP differential + AR murmur + widened mediastinum
CT aortogram is diagnostic standard
Beta-blocker first (rate, dP/dt), then vasodilator
Avoid ACS treatment until dissection excluded
explanation.pearls: Treating dissection like ACS with antiplatelets and anticoagulants is the high-yield exam disconfirming step; the inter-arm BP differential >20 mmHg with an early diastolic murmur in a hypertensive smoker is the picture that must override the ACS reflex.

stem:
A 28-year-old tall thin man is brought into the emergency department after sudden onset right-sided chest pain and shortness of breath 30 minutes ago while playing basketball. He has no past history. On arrival he is alert but pale and diaphoretic. His trachea is shifted to the left, the right hemithorax is hyperresonant with absent breath sounds, and his jugular venous pressure is markedly elevated.

He rapidly deteriorates with hypotension and worsening hypoxia. Pulse 138, BP 84/52 mmHg, RR 32, SpO2 88% on 15 L non-rebreather. A bedside lung ultrasound shows absent lung sliding and a 'barcode sign' on the right.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 138, BP 84/52 mmHg, RR 32, SpO2 88% on 15 L non-rebreather, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Trachea deviated to left, right hemithorax hyperresonant with absent breath sounds, JVP elevated, peripheral cool, no surgical emphysema
data_table.Investigations: Bedside lung ultrasound: absent lung sliding right, barcode sign right; ECG sinus tachycardia; chest X-ray deferred for time-critical management

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Pericardiocentesis under ultrasound guidance as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Cardiac tamponade produces muffled heart sounds, equal breath sounds bilaterally, and pulsus paradoxus; the clinical picture and ultrasound here are pneumothorax, not tamponade. (Source: ATLS 10th Edition)

options.text: Urgent chest X-ray before any intervention as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action as the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis; waiting for imaging delays life-saving decompression and risks cardiac arrest. Bedside ultrasound has already confirmed pneumothorax (absent sliding, barcode sign). (Source: ATLS 10th Edition)

options.text: Endotracheal intubation and positive-pressure ventilation to improve oxygenation as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Positive-pressure ventilation in tension pneumothorax can convert a simple pneumothorax to tension and rapidly worsens haemodynamics; decompression must precede intubation when tension is suspected. (Source: ATLS 10th Edition)

options.text: IV fluids and noradrenaline to support BP while awaiting cardiothoracic surgery as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Fluids and vasopressors do not address the obstructive cause of shock; chest decompression is the only effective intervention. (Source: ATLS 10th Edition)

options.text: Needle decompression of the right hemithorax in the 4th-5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line, followed by intercostal catheter
options.rationale: Tension pneumothorax with haemodynamic compromise requires immediate decompression. AU and ATLS now recommend the 4th-5th intercostal space anterior or mid-axillary line as the preferred site in adults (replacing the 2nd ICS midclavicular site for higher reliability in adult chest wall thickness), followed by formal intercostal catheter for definitive drainage. (Source: ATLS 10th Edition)

explanation.summary: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis identified by haemodynamic compromise with tracheal deviation, ipsilateral hyperresonance and absent breath sounds, raised JVP, and hypoxia. Immediate needle decompression in the 4th-5th ICS anterior or mid-axillary line is followed by formal intercostal catheter. Positive-pressure ventilation before decompression can precipitate cardiac arrest. Lung ultrasound (absent sliding, barcode sign) confirms the diagnosis at the bedside.
key_points: Clinical diagnosis - decompress before imaging
4th-5th ICS anterior/mid-axillary line preferred in adults (ATLS 10)
Avoid positive-pressure ventilation pre-decompression
Formal intercostal catheter follows needle decompression
explanation.pearls: ATLS 10th edition moved adult needle decompression from 2nd ICS midclavicular to 4th-5th ICS anterior/mid-axillary because the lateral chest wall is consistently thinner and the needle more reliably reaches the pleural space.

stem:
A 58-year-old woman is referred to your respiratory clinic with progressive right-sided pleural effusion. She had a thoracic spine T8-T10 fusion 6 weeks ago for traumatic vertebral compression after a motor vehicle accident. She has lost 4 kg since surgery and is mildly breathless on exertion. Diagnostic pleural aspiration today yields 60 mL of milky white fluid.

Fluid biochemistry returns later: pleural total protein 38 g/L (serum 65 g/L), pleural LDH 280 U/L (serum 220 U/L, upper normal 250), pleural pH 7.42, glucose 5.1 mmol/L, cell count 1900 cells/microL with 92% lymphocytes. Pleural cholesterol 1.8 mmol/L. Cytology shows mature lymphocytes with no malignant cells.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 86, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Stony dull right base with reduced breath sounds and decreased tactile fremitus, no clubbing, no peripheral oedema, no palpable lymphadenopathy, thoracic spine surgical scar healing
data_table.Investigations: Pleural fluid: milky white, total protein 38 g/L (serum 65 g/L), LDH 280 U/L (serum 220), pH 7.42, glucose 5.1 mmol/L, cell count 1900 cells/microL with 92% lymphocytes; cholesterol 1.8 mmol/L; cytology mature lymphocytes; CT thorax pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate confirmatory investigation?

options.text: Pleural fluid amylase; raised amylase confirms pancreatic pleural fistula
options.rationale: Raised pleural amylase suggests pancreatic effusion or oesophageal rupture, neither of which is supported by the post-thoracic-surgery context with milky lymphocytic fluid. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease)

options.text: Pleural fluid cholesterol confirms chylothorax if above 5.18 mmol/L
options.rationale: High cholesterol with low triglyceride is the signature of pseudochylothorax (chronic effusions from RA, TB, old empyema), not true chylothorax. Triglyceride is the discriminator for chylothorax. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease)

options.text: Pleural fluid ADA level; above 40 U/L confirms tuberculous pleurisy
options.rationale: ADA is useful in suspected TB pleural effusion but the milky appearance and post-thoracic-surgery context point to chylothorax; ADA does not confirm chylothorax. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease)

options.text: Pleural fluid Gram stain and culture; positive culture confirms empyema
options.rationale: Empyema fluid is purulent with pH below 7.20, glucose below 2.2 mmol/L, and high neutrophils; this fluid has normal pH, normal glucose, and lymphocyte predominance, excluding empyema. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease)

options.text: Send pleural fluid triglyceride; a level above 1.24 mmol/L confirms chylothorax
options.rationale: Milky pleural fluid with high lymphocyte count after thoracic surgery is highly suspicious for chylothorax from thoracic duct injury. Pleural fluid triglyceride above 1.24 mmol/L (110 mg/dL) confirms chylothorax; below 0.56 mmol/L (50 mg/dL) excludes it. The intermediate range requires lipoprotein electrophoresis for chylomicrons. Cholesterol level differentiates pseudochylothorax (cholesterol >5.18 mmol/L with low triglyceride) from true chylothorax. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease)

explanation.summary: Chylothorax presents as milky pleural fluid from thoracic duct injury, often after thoracic, oesophageal, or cardiac surgery. Diagnosis is by pleural fluid triglyceride: above 1.24 mmol/L confirms; below 0.56 mmol/L excludes; intermediate requires chylomicron electrophoresis. Pseudochylothorax (cholesterol >5.18 mmol/L, low triglyceride) is seen in chronic inflammatory effusions. Management is dietary fat restriction with medium-chain triglycerides, octreotide, and thoracic duct ligation or embolisation for refractory cases.
key_points: Pleural triglyceride >1.24 mmol/L = chylothorax
Cholesterol >5.18 mmol/L + low triglyceride = pseudochylothorax
Post-thoracic surgery is a classic chylothorax setting
Treatment: MCT diet, octreotide, then duct ligation/embolisation
explanation.pearls: Pleural cholesterol >5.18 mmol/L with low triglyceride distinguishes pseudochylothorax from true chylothorax - the two milky-effusion entities that look identical at the bedside but have entirely different aetiologies and management.
